nodes.html:
Take the page no one will read,
the one that asks for nothing back.
Let the messy first thoughts bleed —
no red ink, no quick attack.

The critic waits outside the door,
he cannot enter locked-up rooms.
Write the lines you're hiding for,
and watch how quickly fear consumes

itself, when given nothing real
to argue with, to cut, to grade.
There's nothing here to make you feel
small for the words that you have made.

Fold the page, tuck it away.
It did its work, it set you free.
Tomorrow brings another day
to write the things you need to see.

frontmatter.title: Write It and Let It Go

nodes.html:
The voice that says you don't belong,
that says the others know much more —
write it down, make it a song,
a verse to drag onto the shore.

It feels enormous in your head,
a shadow with no shape or name.
But once it's lying there instead,
in ink, it's smaller than your shame.

Now write beneath it, line by line,
one thing you know is true and yours.
Let your own words start to shine
brighter than the closing doors.

Read it back. The voice grows thin,
it cannot hold the weight it had.
You wrote your way to letting in
the truth that you were never bad.

frontmatter.title: Pour the Doubt Onto the Page

nodes.html:
Open a page some stranger wrote
in some quiet, doubting year.
Find the worry that they noted,
find that you are not the only here.

A poet too once felt this small,
once stared at blank and empty light.
Their words still stand against the wall
of every long, uncertain night.

Borrow their courage for a while,
let their rhythm steady yours.
Let their honesty, their style,
open up some inner doors.

You are not alone in this.
The shelf is full of those who felt
exactly what you can't dismiss —
and still they wrote, and still they dealt.

When the whisper says you're fraud,
pick a poem, any one.
Let another voice applaud
the work that's quietly begun.

Their lines were written in the dark,
in rooms as doubtful as your own.
Yet still they left a steady mark,
a signal you are not alone.

Hold the book against your chest,
feel the weight of words gone by.
Let it be your gentle rest,
a shield against the inner cry.

Then put it down, and pick your pen.
Their courage doesn't have to end
with them — pass the torch again,
and let your own words start to mend.

Don't try to build the whole today,
the towering, finished, perfect thing.
Just lay one brick, just find one way
to make one small completion ring.

The doubt that says you can't, won't, shouldn't
grows loud when tasks feel endless, vague.
But finished things, even the smallest,
are proof that you don't have to fake.

Five lines, one shelf, one working part,
something whole, however plain.
Hold it up — that's a start,
that's not nothing, that's a gain.

Stack enough small, finished things
and one day you will turn around
to see the structure that it brings,
built from doubt, but standing, sound.

Perfect is a locked-up room
where nothing ever gets to leave.
It promises, but it's a tomb
for things you worked hard to achieve.

Send the rough one. Send it now.
Let someone see the unfinished part.
The fear that lives behind your brow
grows quiet once you start.

Nothing breaks. The sky stays still.
Someone reads it, someone replies.
You learn that you have something real,
not despite the flaws, but through the tries.

Hidden work helps no one grow,
least of all the one who hides.
Let your rough drafts learn to show
the work that lives on the inside.

Take the page of harsh remarks,
the email, the review, the list.
Cover it in heavy marks
until the noise no longer exists.

Leave a few words standing free,
small and quiet, clear and true.
Let them form a poem to be
the only thing that's left for you.

What you choose to leave unhidden
is the story you now tell.
The rest stays buried, dark, forbidden,
you decide what rings the bell.

A blank page once held you small.
Now it's yours to fill with light.
Paint over doubt and watch it fall
into the quiet, settled night.

Your doubt has never had a shape,
it floats as shadow, vague and wide.
So draw it small, give it escape
from the corners where it hides.

Maybe it's a deflated balloon,
a bird beneath a heavy crown,
a grumpy, round, cartoon buffoon
with too many eyes, looking down.

Cut it, fold it, glue it, bend it,
make it something you can hold.
A little thing you can pretend
isn't quite as big as it's been told.

Now it sits there on your desk,
small and silly, made by you.
The voice that felt like such a test
is just a shape — and shapes can move.

When the panic tightens in,
don't think your way back out.
Open up and let begin
a low and steady sound.

Hum a note, hold it long,
feel it move through chest and jaw.
This isn't about being strong,
it's about following a law

older than your worried mind:
breath and rhythm, in and out,
calm the body, ease the bind,
quiet down the inner shout.

Sing badly, sing off-key,
no one's grading what you do.
Let the sound move through and free
the place where panic used to live in you.

Step outside the grid of walls,
the rooms that measure every sound.
Find a place where no one calls
your singing wrong, or out of bound.

Under trees, the air is wide.
No reviews, no scoring sheet.
A leaf does not stand judged with pride
or fear that it's not quite complete.

Let your voice go where it wants,
loud or soft, off-key, unplanned.
The forest doesn't keep accounts
of every note, or where you stand.

Come back in, carrying that ease,
the feeling that you, too, can grow
without the constant need to please —
a song that no one scored, but you let go.

Go back to something old you wrote,
the early lines, the clumsy start.
Read it now, and take note
of the distance, and the art.

You winced back then at every word,
sure that it gave you away.
Now it reads like something heard
from someone finding their own way.

The gap between then and now
is not luck, it's not pretend.
It's the slow and steady how
of someone who did not stop or bend.

So when today feels just as raw,
remember what this reading shows —
the page that scared you, flaw by flaw,
is proof of how a writer grows.

When the page won't move, and your
shoulders climb up toward your ears,
stand up, leave the desk, and walk —
the answer isn't always there.

Your feet know something thinking doesn't,
a rhythm older than your doubt.
Step by step, the knot loosens,
the noise begins to thin out.

You don't have to solve it now,
or prove that you were right to pause.
Just walk until you notice how
the tightness eases, without cause.

Come back to the page renewed,
not because you forced a fix,
but because you simply moved —
and sometimes that's the only trick.

Turn the music up too loud
and let your body answer first.
No one's watching in this crowd —
it's just you, and a steady burst.

The mind that judges every step
forgets itself when hips take lead.
You're not performing, you're not kept
to counts of four, to forms, to creed.

Let your arms go where they want,
let your knees forget the rules.
This is not a test, a vaunt —
just a body, loosening its tools.

When the song winds down to quiet,
notice what has come undone.
You danced your way out of the riot
of thoughts — and for now, you've won.

Chop the onion, stir the pot,
follow steps someone wrote with care.
For an hour, you don't need a lot —
just hands, and heat, and being there.

If it's not exactly right,
a little salt, a little more —
no one grades you on this night,
the only judge is at your door.

Something real comes from this work,
something warm you get to share.
No résumé, no hidden quirk,
just proof that you showed up, and care.

Sit down now and eat what's made,
let the taste be its own applause.
You built this meal, course by course laid,
small, and whole, and worth the pause.

Plant the seed and walk away.
It won't grow faster watched, or rushed.
There's no metric for today,
no deadline that can be crushed.

The roots are working underground,
unseen, unmeasured, out of sight.
Competence grows the same way — sound,
slow, and steady, out of sight.

You won't see results this week,
maybe not even this whole season.
But tend it anyway — don't seek
proof before you have a reason.

One day there's a bud, a shoot,
small evidence of all that's grown.
You didn't force it. You took root.
And patience was a skill you'd shown.

Pick up the camera, or your phone,
and look for one small thing today —
the light on brick, a leaf alone,
a shadow stretching across the way.

You don't need a grand subject,
no skyline, no dramatic scene.
Just notice what you might reject
on any other day, unseen.

Frame it. Take the shot. That's all.
No filter needed, no big claim.
You looked at something ordinary,
and found it worthy of a frame.

This is the practice: paying attention,
not performance, not a show.
And noticing is its own dimension
of work that matters. Now you know.

Take a pen, take any paper,
and make a mark with no plan in mind.
Not a sketch, not a project, not a caper —
just a line, then another, intertwined.

Let it loop, let it tangle,
let it wander off the page's edge.
There's no angle here to wrangle,
no client, no judge, no pledge.

If it's ugly, that's allowed.
If it's nothing, that's allowed too.
This is just your hand, unbowed,
moving because it wanted to.

Look down at what you've made,
a mess of lines, and that's just fine.
Some part of you that felt afraid
just spent ten minutes feeling fine.

Open the notebook, write the date,
then write whatever's in your head.
No structure here, no need to wait
for something wise or well-bred.

Write about the meeting that went sideways,
the thing you wish you'd said instead.
Write about the long, strange highways
your thoughts took before you went to bed.

No one will grade this. No one's reading.
This page exists for only you.
It's not output, it's not pleading,
it's just a place to work things through.

Close the notebook. Feel the weight
of what you carried, now set down.
Tomorrow there's a fresh blank slate,
and today's noise won't follow you around.

Take old magazines, old letters,
torn-up paper, ticket stubs.
You don't need to be your betters,
just scissors, glue, and what there was.

Cut a shape from something printed,
move it somewhere it didn't belong.
Nothing here was ever meant
to fit together — but make it strong.

Glue it down. Let the pieces
find a new and stranger sense.
Every edge that overlaps releases
something fixed, something tense.

You took the scraps that were around
and built something that wasn't there.
Everything worth finding is found
in pieces — rearranged with care.

Pick up the instrument, the controller,
the deck of cards, the worn-out ball.
Not to get better, not to grow taller
in skill — just to play, that's all.

Remember when this was the point,
before the lessons, before the score,
before you felt the need to anoint
every hour with something more.

Play badly. Play just for the feel
of fingers, hands, or feet in motion.
There's nothing here that's at stake or real
except a small, returning notion:

that joy doesn't need a reason,
that play was never meant to prove.
Let this be an offseason
from everything you have to improve.

frontmatter.title: Pick It Up Just to Play

nodes.html:
Slip into the water slow,
feel the world above go quiet.
Down here, there's nowhere left to go
but forward, through the blue and white.

Your thoughts can't follow past the surface,
they wait up there, dry and loud.
Down here it's just breath and purpose,
stroke by stroke, uncrowned.

No one critiques a length of pool.
No one's watching for mistakes.
The water doesn't call you a fool,
it just holds you while it takes

you forward, lap by steady lap,
until the noise upstairs is gone.
Climb out, towel off, close the gap
between the panic and the calm.

Find the thing you think you're faking,
the skill you're sure is mostly luck,
and explain it — slow, unshaking —
to someone who's just starting up.

Watch the words come out in order,
watch the pieces start to fit.
What felt like standing on a border
turns out to be a thing you've split

into steps that someone else can follow.
That's not luck. That's not chance.
That's the kind of thing that's hollow
only if you never glance

back at how much you actually carry.
Teaching shows you what you know.
The fraud who whispered you should worry
just watched you help someone else grow.

frontmatter.title: Say It Out Loud to Someone Else
